title: California's electric train push cuts 89% carcinogenic pollutants
description: The study estimates that this reduction in black carbon will significantly lower cancer risk for people regularly exposed to the pollutants.
bodyText: Bay Area commuters traveling between San Francisco and San Jose may now be breathing significantly cleaner air, thanks to Caltrain’s recent transition from diesel-powered trains to electric. A new study shows the switch resulted in a dramatic drop in toxic air pollutants on board trains and around major transit hubs. According to researchers from UC Berkeley, the introduction of electric trains led to an average 89 percent reduction in black carbon (a carcinogenic component of diesel exhaust) in the air that riders breathed during the journey after Caltrain installed its electric fleet in the late summer of 2024. “The transition from diesel to electric trains occurred over just a few weeks, and yet we saw the same drop in black carbon concentrations in the station as California cities achieved from 30 years of clean air regulations,” said study senior author Joshua Apte. “It really adds to the case for electrifying the many other rail systems in the U.S. that still use old, poorly regulated diesel locomotives.” Caltrain, the busiest commuter rail system in the western U.S., retired all 29 of its diesel locomotives over a six-week period between August and September 2024. They were replaced with 23 modern electric trains as part of a $2.44 billion modernization initiative that began in 2017. What made this transition unique was not just its scale, but its speed—and the immediate impact it had on air quality. The researchers started tracking the change as soon as electric trains came into operation. While observing the pungent diesel odor and the irritating sounds still present at the San Francisco station, Apte understood that they had a unique opportunity to observe rapid changes to the environment. “I was stunned at how much the station smelled like diesel smoke and how noisy it was from the racket of diesel locomotives idling away at the platforms, dumping smoke out into the community,” Apte said. “A light bulb went off my head — I realized this would all be going away in a few weeks.” With the support of Caltrain, the research group placed sensors on the portable monitors to measure black carbon at various stations and on the train, which allowed them to assess pollution levels remotely. In the subsequent month, they recorded an astounding reduction in black carbon exposure for all participants— train passengers as well as the crew. “A lot of these transitions happen pretty slowly. This one happened in a blink of an eye,” Apte said. “We had the unique opportunity to capture the ancillary public health benefits.” The study estimates that this reduction in black carbon will significantly lower cancer risk for people regularly exposed to the pollutants. “If you think about this in the context of the whole of the U.S., where we have millions of people commuting by rail every day, that’s hundreds of cases of cancer that could be prevented each year,” said Samuel Cliff, a postdoctoral scholar at UC Berkeley. “California has long-term plans to electrify most of its rail systems, but this shows that we shouldn’t be waiting another 25 years to get it done. We should be speeding it up,” he concludes. Srishti started out as an editor for academic journal articles before switching to reportage. With a keen interest in all things science, Srishti is particularly drawn to beats covering medicine, sustainable architecture, gene studies, and bioengineering. When she isn't elbows-deep in research for her next feature, Srishti enjoys reading contemporary fiction and chasing after her cats.
